This prompted the Germans to strengthen the stern structures on all German capital ships.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallard1990215-191)

### Subsequent expeditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=German_battleship_Bismarck&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Subsequent expeditions")]

In June 2001, Deep Ocean Expeditions, partnered with [Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution](/wiki/Woods_Hole_Oceanographic_Institution "Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution"), conducted another investigation of the wreck. The researchers used Russian-built [mini-submarines](/wiki/Submersible "Submersible"). William N. Lange, a Woods Hole expert, stated, "You see a large number of shell holes in the superstructure and deck, but not that many along the side, and none below the waterline."[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''New_York_Times'',_"Visiting_Bismarck"-193) The expedition found no penetrations in the main armoured belt, above or below the waterline. The examiners noted several long gashes in the hull, but attributed these to impact on the sea floor.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''New_York_Times'',_"Visiting_Bismarck"-193)

An Anglo-American expedition in July 2001 was funded by a British TV channel. The team used the volcano—the only one in that area—to locate the wreck. Using [ROVs](/wiki/Remotely_operated_underwater_vehicle "Remotely operated underwater vehicle") to film the hull, the team concluded that the ship had sunk due to combat damage. Expedition leader [David Mearns](/wiki/David_Mearns "David Mearns") claimed significant gashes had been found in the hull: "My feeling is that those holes were probably lengthened by the slide, but initiated by torpedoes".[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''New_York_Times'',_"Visiting_Bismarck"-193)

The 2002 documentary *[Expedition: Bismarck](/wiki/Expedition:_Bismarck "Expedition: Bismarck")*, directed by [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron") and filmed in May–June 2002 using smaller and more agile [Mir submersibles](/wiki/Mir_(submersible) "Mir (submersible)"), reconstructed the events leading to the sinking. These provided the first interior shots.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''New_York_Times'',_"Visiting_Bismarck"-193) Unlike the Ballard expedition, Cameron's investigation was able to examine parts of the sides of the hull. When *Bismarck* hit the bottom bow first, massive "hydraulic outburst" separated much of the shell plating of the hull along the line where it joined the bottom of the armour belt. The decks under the armour deck have been compressed by 3 to 4 metres.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200242-194) The most extensive damage, including the gashes Mearns observed, was thus due to impact of the hull with the ocean floor as opposed to directly due to battle damage.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200247-195)

Although around 719 large calibre shells were fired at *Bismarck* that morning, Cameron's survey noted only two instances where the 320 mm main side belt armour had actually been fully penetrated in the visible parts of the hull.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200230-196) These were both on the starboard side amidships. One hole is forward of the 320 mm displaced armour belt. In the second case the explosion dislodged a rectangular segment of the 320 mm armour. Instead, the expedition argues long range plunging fire hitting the deck was almost completely responsible for the damage that directly contributed to the sinking of the ship.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200230–31-197) The later close-range shelling (including by secondary armament) "devastated the superstructure and exposed parts of the hull above the waterline, and caused massive casualties", but contributed little to the sinking of the ship.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200251-172) British gunnery accuracy was "mediocre at best", partially due to the "miserable" firing conditions and the ship's list to port, with only around 10% of fired medium calibre shots hitting.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200232–34-198)

The Cameron report stated that "The devastation caused by the shellfire combined with the effects of several torpedo hits to overwhelm and defeat the *Bismarck*, causing the ship to begin sinking due to uncontrollable progressive flooding. The German crew sped the inevitable demise of their ship by initiating scuttling measures."[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200220-199) It suggests the torpedoes may have increased the subsequent extensive damage to the ship when it hit the ocean floor.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200233-177) In some cases the torpedo blasts had failed to shatter the torpedo bulkheads.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''New_York_Times'',_"Visiting_Bismarck"-193) and some hits claimed may have been torpedoes that exploded prematurely due to the heavy seas.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200231-200) However the locations of other hits were buried in mud or were impossible to distinguish due to the extent of overall damage to the ship.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200233-177) Overall the report disputed Mearns' claim that scuttling was irrelevant to the timing of the sinking.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200242-194)

Despite their sometimes differing viewpoints, these experts generally agree that *Bismarck* would have eventually foundered if the Germans had not scuttled her first. Ballard estimated that *Bismarck* could still have floated for at least a day when the British vessels ceased gunfire, but before Bismarck was torpedoed by *Dorsetshire*, and could have been captured by the Royal Navy, a position supported by Ludovic Kennedy (who was serving on the destroyer [HMS *Tartar*](/wiki/HMS_Tartar_(F43) "HMS Tartar (F43)") at the time). Kennedy stated, "That she would have foundered eventually there can be little doubt; but the scuttling ensured that it was sooner rather than later."[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallard1990214–215-189) The Cameron expedition's report asserts "Bismarck unquestionably would have sunk due to progressive flooding hours after the battle ended".[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECameronDulinGarzkeJurens200250-160) In Mearns' subsequent book *Hood and Bismarck*, he conceded that scuttling "may have hastened the inevitable, but only by a matter of minutes."[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''New_York_Times'',_"Visiting_Bismarck"-193) Ballard later concluded that "As far as I was concerned, the British had sunk the ship regardless of who delivered the final blow."[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallard2008111-201)
